topic: A person learns their childhood bully became a therapist
raw_output:
[story 1]

The polished oak of the waiting room felt cold beneath my fingertips. I kept tracing the grain, a nervous habit. My palms were slick. When the door finally opened, I saw him. Liam. He looked… different. The familiar sneer was gone, replaced by a calm, almost disconcerting attentiveness. He wore a soft grey sweater instead of his usual worn denim jacket. My stomach clenched, a tight, unpleasant knot forming just beneath my ribs.

"Alex?" His voice was lower than I remembered, smooth, like worn velvet. It was the same timbre, the same slight rasp, but threaded now with something unfamiliar—a measured cadence. I shuffled forward, my movements jerky. I managed a weak, "Hi."

He offered a small, genuine smile. It didn't reach his eyes, which were focused entirely on me. "Come in, please. Have a seat." The air in the room seemed to thicken, pressing in on me. Every instinct screamed at me to bolt, but my feet remained rooted. I sank onto the uncomfortable chair, pulling my knees up to my chest, trying to make myself small. The silence stretched, heavy and accusatory, punctuated only by the faint hum of the ventilation system. I just stared at the pattern on the carpet, waiting for the inevitable confrontation, or perhaps, the inevitable return of the old dynamic.

[story 2]

I kept catching my reflection in the glass of the hallway. My face was pale, the skin stretched tight over my cheekbones. My breath hitched every time I heard footsteps approaching. It was him. Liam. The memory of his sharp words, the way he’d deliberately push me until I stumbled, flooded my senses. Now, he was sitting behind a large, uncluttered desk, leaning forward, his posture radiating an unnerving control.

"So," he began, his tone even, almost pedagogical. "Tell me what brought you in today, Alex."

My throat felt constricted, dry and useless. I swallowed hard, the movement catching in my chest. The words felt enormous, clumsy, like stones I was trying to push up a hill. I opened my mouth, but only a shallow puff of air escaped. My hands clenched into fists inside my pockets, the knuckles turning white. I could feel the scrutiny, an intense, unwavering gaze that seemed to dissect every insecurity I possessed. It wasn't anger I felt; it was a profound sense of exposure, as if every secret I’d ever harbored was already laid bare for his inspection.

[story 3]

The scent of old paper and something faintly citrusy hung in the air of the office. I sat rigidly on the edge of the sofa, my spine straight enough to snap. Every time Liam shifted in his chair, the subtle sound of the leather creaking sent a jolt through me. I watched his hands—long, elegant, entirely unlike the rough hands that used to grip my backpack straps. They moved with deliberate slowness as he adjusted a stack of files.

"It takes a lot of courage to seek this kind of help, Alex," he murmured, not looking up immediately. When he finally did, his eyes met mine. There was a flicker there—a ghost of the old antagonism, quickly smothered by a practiced empathy. My jaw tightened. I wanted to argue, to lash out with the familiar venom I used to deploy, but the words wouldn't form. Instead, a wave of intense vulnerability washed over me, making my vision swim slightly. I pressed my lips together, fighting the urge to pull away, to flee the suffocating proximity of this man who used to be my worst nightmare.
